title: How does this compare to other towns?
intro: Lifetime, Town-subsidized retiree health coverage isn't unique to Riverhead — it's standard practice across Long Island towns, all of which face the same math: a pay-as-you-go promise that grows every year retirees live longer and premiums rise. The Empire Center for Public Policy publishes a per-municipality OPEB liability comparison; here's where Riverhead lands next to its Suffolk County neighbors.
methodologyNote: This table uses the Empire Center's OPEB Liabilities Tool (published Dec. 2020), which reports a 'municipal net liability' and a per-resident 'share' — a different snapshot, and possibly a different accounting scope, than the $152,597,117 OPEB figure sourced from Riverhead's 2023 audit used elsewhere on this site. Treat this as a same-source, apples-to-apples ranking across towns rather than a literal match to the 2023 figure.
ranking: Riverhead ranks 4th-highest of these 10 Suffolk towns on a per-resident basis — behind the smaller East End towns (Shelter Island, East Hampton, Southampton, which spread a similar-sized liability across far fewer residents) and ahead of the larger western-Suffolk towns (Brookhaven, Islip, Huntington), which have much bigger populations to spread the same kind of cost across.
whyItVaries: Population size is the single biggest driver of the per-resident figure: East End towns are small, so even a modest total liability produces a high per-resident number; Brookhaven and Islip are Suffolk's largest towns by population, so a much bigger total liability still nets out to a small per-resident share.

How generous each town's retiree health benefit is (lifetime full-family coverage vs. a buyback/opt-out option, contribution-sharing rules, how many years of service vest the benefit) varies by union contract and changes over time — Riverhead's own CSEA 2026-2029 contract, for example, added new retiree buyback amounts for employees who decline coverage.

Workforce mix matters: towns with larger police forces tend to carry more of this liability, since police and fire unions have historically negotiated stronger retiree-health terms than general civilian units.
empireContext: For scale, New York's own statewide NYSHIP Participating-Employer premium (the benchmark plan Riverhead's coverage tracks) runs about $1,611/month ($19,337/yr) for individual coverage and $3,664/month ($43,965/yr) for family coverage for a non-Medicare retiree, or about $596/month (~$7,157/yr) once a retiree becomes Medicare-primary. Riverhead's own blended ~$17,000/retiree/year (2023 audit) sits between those figures — consistent with a retiree population that's a mix of pre-Medicare and Medicare-eligible ages.
sources: Empire Center for Public Policy, OPEB Liabilities Tool (empirecenter.org/publications/opeb-liabilities-tool/), published Dec. 9, 2020.
NYS Civil Service, NYSHIP Participating Employer premium rates (cs.ny.gov/employee-benefits/pa-market/pe_rates.cfm) and Participating Agency rates (rates.cfm).
Town of Riverhead 2023 Audited Basic Financial Statements (OPEB note).
